cards.title: Azure SQL Database Tiers and Purchasing Models
cards.teaser: Azure SQL Database is a fully managed PaaS SQL Server — no VMs, no patching — but you have to pick the right purchasing model and tier before you provision, because switching later is painful.

runs.text: Azure SQL Database removes OS and engine management from your plate. You connect a standard SQL Server connection string, and Azure handles patching, backups, and high availability under the hood.
Two purchasing models govern how you pay for compute:

items.text: a bundled abstraction of CPU, memory, and I/O. Predictable, easy to reason about for simple workloads. Three tiers: Basic, Standard, Premium. If you can't characterize your workload precisely, start here.
explicit CPU and memory allocation. Required for Hyperscale. Lets you pick the hardware generation and apply the Azure Hybrid Benefit if you own existing SQL Server licenses.

runs.text: Three vCore service tiers:

items.text: standard SSD-backed storage, 99.99% SLA. Covers the majority of workloads.
local SSD, built-in read replica, sub-millisecond I/O. The right call when you need the lowest latency or a free readable secondary.

decoupled storage and compute architecture. Scales storage up to 100 TB without downtime. Adds up to four read replicas. Suited for large databases with unpredictable growth — think 10 TB+ logs or analytics offloads.

runs.text: The
Serverless

compute tier (vCore only) auto-pauses after a configurable idle period and bills per-second when active. At roughly $0.0002 per vCore-second, it's the right choice for dev databases or apps with bursty, infrequent traffic — not for anything with a consistent load, where a provisioned tier is cheaper.
If your database sits idle more than 50% of the day, Serverless almost always wins on cost. Enable auto-pause with a 60-minute delay to start.
Think of DTU as a "meal plan" where the cafeteria bundles your calories for you, and vCore as ordering a la carte — same food, different billing philosophy.

cards.title: Azure SQL Elastic Pools
cards.teaser: Elastic pools let a fleet of databases share a single DTU or vCore budget — the right pattern when you have many databases that spike at different times and you don't want to overprovision each one.

runs.text: Individual databases need to be sized for peak load. If you have 50 tenant databases, most of which idle while a few are active, you end up paying peak prices for 50 databases simultaneously — most of that capacity wasted at any given moment.

Elastic pools solve this with a shared resource model. You allocate a pool of DTUs or vCores, and all databases in the pool draw from that shared budget. The database currently running the heavy query gets more resources; the idle ones get less.
When elastic pools beat individual databases:

items.text: where each tenant has their own database but usage is rarely synchronized.

runs.text: Two limits matter for pool sizing:

items.text: the ceiling on total compute available to all databases simultaneously. Size this for your peak aggregate load, not your peak single-database load.
caps how much any single database can consume. Prevents one noisy tenant from starving others. Set this based on your largest expected single-database spike.

runs.text: The crossover point is roughly: if the sum of individually-sized databases exceeds the pool price by 20%+, pool them. Azure's portal shows a cost estimator when you're configuring the pool.
Elastic pools don't help if your databases spike at the same time — a shared pool under synchronized load is just a smaller individual database that serves everyone worse.
An elastic pool is like a shared office internet connection — fine when different people are active at different hours, but everyone suffers when the whole team joins a video call at 9 AM.

cards.title: Cosmos DB Fundamentals
cards.teaser: Cosmos DB is Azure's globally distributed, multi-model NoSQL service — if you need sub-10ms reads anywhere on the planet and you can live without full ACID transactions, this is the database to reach for.

runs.text: Cosmos DB stores JSON documents and distributes them globally across Azure regions. You can replicate to any number of regions and serve reads locally from the nearest one, with typical read latencies under 10ms at the 99th percentile.
It is multi-model — meaning the same underlying engine is exposed through five different APIs:

items.text: document queries using a SQL-like syntax. The native API and the one with the deepest feature support.
wire-compatible with MongoDB 4.0+. Lift existing Mongo apps with a connection string change.
CQL-compatible. Useful for migrating from Apache Cassandra.
graph traversal for connected data (social graphs, recommendation engines).
key-value storage compatible with Azure Table Storage. The simplest model.

runs.text: A few numbers worth knowing:

items.text: the maximum size of a single logical partition. Exceed this and writes fail. Your partition key design must distribute data across many partitions.

the throughput currency of Cosmos DB. One RU represents the cost of reading a 1 KB document. All operations — reads, writes, queries — are expressed in RUs. You provision RU/s at the container or database level.
by default, every field in every document is indexed. You don't define a schema upfront. You can exclude fields from indexing to reduce write overhead.

runs.text: Cosmos DB charges for both storage and provisioned RU/s, even when idle. Serverless mode (pay-per-RU, no provisioned floor) exists for dev and low-traffic workloads — but it has a 1 TB storage ceiling.
Cosmos DB is like a vending machine network where every city has a local machine stocked identically — you always get your snack fast, but the restocking logic is what makes it complex.

cards.title: Cosmos DB Consistency Levels
cards.teaser: Cosmos DB offers five consistency levels on a spectrum from "always correct" to "always fast" — and choosing the wrong one either kills your performance or breaks your app in subtle ways.

runs.text: Distributed databases can't have strong consistency, high availability, and low latency simultaneously — the CAP theorem says pick two. Cosmos DB externalizes this tradeoff as five named consistency levels you set per-account (or override per-request).
From strongest to weakest:

items.text: reads always return the latest committed write. Writes replicate synchronously to a quorum across all regions before acknowledgment. Latency equals your slowest region. Only available in single-region accounts or accounts with a single write region.
reads lag behind writes by at most K versions or T seconds (you set both). Useful when you need a known staleness bound — think financial dashboards that can be 5 seconds behind.

within a single client session, reads see your own writes. Across sessions, reads may see slightly stale data. This covers the vast majority of app scenarios — a user always sees their own changes, and other users see eventually consistent data.
reads never see out-of-order writes. If writes are A then B then C, a reader might see A or A+B but never A+C without B.
no ordering guarantees. Maximum throughput, minimum cost. Suitable for non-critical aggregations, logs, or telemetry that gets reconciled later.

runs.text: The cost tradeoff: moving from Eventual toward Strong approximately doubles the RU cost of reads and increases write latency. Session consistency delivers the best balance for most CRUD applications.
Strong consistency is incompatible with multi-region write accounts. If you need both global writes and strong reads, you'll need application-level conflict resolution.

Choosing a consistency level is like deciding whether every branch office sees the org chart updated in real time, on a 5-minute delay, or "eventually" — with each step toward real-time costing more bandwidth and coordination.

cards.title: Cosmos DB Partition Key Design
cards.teaser: The partition key is the single most consequential schema decision in Cosmos DB — a bad choice causes hot partitions that throttle writes and cap your throughput no matter how many RU/s you provision.

runs.text: Cosmos DB scales horizontally by distributing data across physical partitions. Each logical partition is identified by a partition key value and is capped at 10 GB. Throughput is distributed evenly across physical partitions — so if all your writes land on one key, you're effectively single-threaded.
Hot partition anti-patterns to avoid:

items.text: (values: "active", "inactive") means all active records land on one partition. Same problem with boolean fields or small enums.
if your ID is a timestamp or an auto-incrementing integer, recent writes pile onto the latest partition. Classic for IoT sensor data.
works fine for average users, but if one user generates 10x more writes, their partition becomes the bottleneck.

runs.text: Design for an even spread:

items.text: (thousands to millions of distinct values) and
often works well if tenants are roughly equal in activity.
(e.g., month) to avoid sequential hot spots.

runs.text: The
synthetic partition key
pattern: concatenate two or more fields into a single key value to increase cardinality when no single field distributes well on its own. Example:
/customerId_productCategory

Cross-partition query cost: querying across partitions fans out to every physical partition and multiplies RU consumption. A query that costs 5 RUs on a single partition may cost 500 RUs if it spans 100 partitions.
Partition key design is like choosing which letter of the alphabet to use as a filing system — if half your words start with "S", that drawer overflows while the others sit empty.

cards.title: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
cards.teaser: Azure's managed PostgreSQL is the right move for teams already running Postgres who want PaaS convenience without giving up the features and extensions they depend on.

runs.text: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server is the current-generation managed Postgres offering. The older Single Server is deprecated — if you're starting today, you start here.
Key capabilities worth knowing:

items.text: PostgreSQL 14 through 16. Minor version updates are automatic; major version upgrades require an in-place migration or export/import.
standby in a different availability zone, automatic failover in 60–120 seconds. No manual intervention. Costs roughly 2x a single-server deployment.
up to five replicas in the same or different regions. Replicas are asynchronous — they don't count toward your write availability SLA.

built-in, enabled with a checkbox. Critical for serverless apps or any workload where connection count spikes — raw Postgres connections are expensive, and a managed pooler removes the need to run your own PgBouncer sidecar.
, and 50+ others. Check the allowed-extensions list before assuming a given extension is available — not every Postgres extension is permitted in a managed environment.

runs.text: Storage autogrows to prevent out-of-disk failures, but you pay per GB provisioned. Compute can be stopped during off-hours (useful for dev environments) and billed only while running.
pgBouncer in Transaction mode is the right default for most web apps. Session mode is needed if your app uses prepared statements, advisory locks, or
LISTEN/NOTIFY

Flexible Server is like renting a fully maintained Postgres box where the landlord handles backups, OS updates, and HA failover, but you still control the schema, the extensions, and the query planner.

cards.title: Azure Cache for Redis
cards.teaser: Azure Cache for Redis is a managed Redis service that handles the three most common caching problems — session storage, database offloading, and real-time leaderboards — without you running a Redis cluster yourself.

runs.text: Redis is an in-memory data structure store. Azure's managed version removes the operational overhead of running Redis on VMs while adding zone redundancy and enterprise-grade security.
Three tiers cover different needs:

items.text: single node, no SLA, no replication. Dev and test only. Don't run production workloads on Basic.
two-node primary/replica setup, 99.9% SLA. The right default for production caching and session storage.

adds Redis Cluster (horizontal sharding across multiple nodes), data persistence (RDB snapshots and AOF), VNet injection for private network access, and geo-replication. Required when you need more than 53 GB of cache capacity or sub-millisecond P99 latency at high throughput.

runs.text: Practical use cases and the Redis data structure that serves each:

items.text: string keys mapped to serialized session JSON. TTL-based expiration handles session cleanup automatically.
cache expensive query results with a TTL matching your acceptable staleness window. Common pattern: cache for 60 seconds, invalidate on write.
Redis Streams or Pub/Sub for lightweight async messaging between services. Not a replacement for Service Bus or Event Hubs, but fits low-latency in-process fan-out.
) deliver O(log N) ranked access. INCR-based counters with TTL implement sliding window rate limiting in a few lines.

runs.text: For caches larger than 53 GB, the Premium tier's Redis Cluster distributes keys across shards. Azure also offers the
Enterprise
tier (powered by Redis Stack) for active geo-replication with sub-second cross-region sync.
Cache-aside is the most common pattern, but it introduces a cache miss window on cold start and after invalidation. Pre-warm caches before a traffic spike, not after.
Redis is like the whiteboard in the office — not the source of truth, but far faster to read from than going back to the filing cabinet every time.

cards.title: Azure Synapse Analytics
cards.teaser: Synapse is Azure's unified analytics platform — it combines a cloud data warehouse, serverless SQL over data lakes, Spark compute, and pipeline orchestration under one service, so you don't have to stitch them together yourself.

runs.text: Synapse Analytics merges capabilities that previously required separate products. You get four compute engines from a single workspace:

items.text: a massively parallel processing (MPP) data warehouse. You provision compute in Data Warehouse Units (DWUs). Scale up before a heavy ETL load, scale down or pause on weekends. Best for structured, historical reporting at petabyte scale.

ad-hoc T-SQL queries directly over files in Azure Data Lake Storage (Parquet, CSV, JSON). No provisioning — pay per TB of data scanned. Right for exploration, building external tables, or one-off data quality checks.
managed Spark clusters for Python, Scala, or SQL-based data engineering and ML feature preparation. Spin up in ~2 minutes; shut down automatically when idle.
built-in data integration (similar to Azure Data Factory) for orchestrating ingestion, transformation, and movement between sources.

runs.text: DWU sizing guidance: start at DW100c for dev/test, and run a load test before committing to a production tier. Compute and storage are separated — pausing compute stops the billing clock without dropping your data.
Synapse vs Databricks:

Synapse integrates better with the Azure-native stack (ADLS, Power BI, Purview) and is lower overhead for teams already invested in T-SQL. Databricks offers more advanced MLOps, Delta Lake features, and a stronger open-source ecosystem. For pure data engineering or unified ML pipelines, Databricks often wins. For SQL-centric BI teams on Azure, Synapse is the simpler path.
Serverless SQL pool costs nothing to maintain — you only pay when a query runs. It's worth setting up early as a lightweight query interface to your data lake even before you need a dedicated pool.

Synapse is like a research institute where the same building houses the library (data lake), the main computer (dedicated pool), a hot desk for quick questions (serverless SQL), and a lab for experiments (Spark) — same ID badge gets you into all of them.

cards.title: Azure SQL Managed Instance
cards.teaser: SQL Managed Instance is the SQL Server lift-and-shift target — near-100% compatibility with on-premises SQL Server, but running as a PaaS service inside your VNet with no VM management.

runs.text: Azure SQL Database and SQL Managed Instance both run SQL Server under the hood, but they serve different migration scenarios. SQL Database is cloud-native and has dropped some legacy SQL Server features by design. SQL Managed Instance preserves almost everything — which is why it costs more.
Features SQL MI has that Azure SQL Database lacks:

items.text: scheduled jobs using T-SQL or SSIS packages, exactly as on-premises.
cross-database queries to external data sources, including on-prem SQL servers.
execute .NET assemblies inside the database engine.
asynchronous message queuing within SQL Server.
within the same managed instance, you can query across databases without a distributed transaction coordinator.
Database Mail, CDC, and Change Tracking:
all present, all configured the same way as on-prem.

runs.text: The key architectural difference: SQL MI is
VNet-native

. It is always deployed inside a dedicated subnet in your VNet. There is no public endpoint option for the managed instance itself (though a proxy can be configured). This is intentional — it models the security posture of on-premises SQL Server deployment.

Pricing reflects the compatibility premium. SQL MI is roughly 2–3x more expensive than an equivalently-sized Azure SQL Database vCore instance. Before choosing MI over SQL DB, audit which SQL Server features your app actually uses. If the answer is "none of the above," SQL Database saves you real money.

Use the SQL Server Migration Assistant (SSMA) or Azure Database Migration Service to run a compatibility assessment before committing to MI vs SQL DB. Many apps discover they don't need MI's extra features once they check.

SQL Managed Instance is like a pre-furnished apartment in a managed building — everything from your old house fits and works, you don't own the building infrastructure, but the rent is higher than a studio built for modern living.

cards.title: Choosing the Right Azure Database Service
cards.teaser: Azure has seven database services and each solves a different problem — the wrong choice is fixable, but only after a migration that costs more than the decision took to make.

runs.text: Most Azure database decisions map cleanly to a small set of questions. Work through this decision tree before you provision anything:
Is your data relational?

items.text: . Pick General Purpose + vCore for most workloads. Serverless if load is bursty and idle time is high.
Yes, and you're migrating an existing SQL Server application:
. Run a compatibility assessment first — if MI features aren't needed, SQL Database saves money.
Yes, and you're running PostgreSQL or MySQL:
Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server
. Both are managed, zone-redundant, and support read replicas.

runs.text: Is your data non-relational / document / key-value / graph?

items.text: Need global distribution and single-digit millisecond reads from multiple regions:
. Pick the API that matches your existing data model (SQL for new projects, MongoDB if migrating Mongo).
Pure key-value with simple access patterns:
depending on whether you need persistence or sub-millisecond in-memory speed.

runs.text: Is your workload analytics / reporting over large data sets?

items.text: Ad-hoc queries over a data lake without provisioning compute:
Complex data engineering, ML pipelines, open-source ecosystem: consider Azure Databricks alongside or instead of Synapse.

runs.text: Do you need caching or session storage?

items.text: . Standard tier for most apps; Premium if you need clustering, persistence, or VNet injection.

runs.text: The most expensive mistake is picking Cosmos DB because it sounds scalable, then discovering your data is relational and you need joins. The second most expensive is picking SQL MI when SQL Database would have worked fine.

When in doubt, start with Azure SQL Database. It covers the most workloads, has the lowest operational overhead, and costs the least. You can migrate up to MI or Cosmos DB later if requirements change.

Picking a database service is like picking a vehicle — a sports car (Cosmos DB) is genuinely faster in certain conditions, but if you're mostly doing grocery runs (CRUD with joins), you needed the sedan (SQL Database) the whole time.
